3 min read
Just now

--

I spent weeks trying to understand KAN. Here’s the explanation I wish I’d found on day one.
KANs, or Kolmogorov–Arnold Networks, are a newer kind of machine-learning model designed to be easier to understand than regular neural networks. They are inspired by a famous math theorem about breaking complicated functions into simpler one-variable functions, and the modern KAN paper was first submitted in April 2024.
Enough history let's really understand what KAN is?
KAN stands for Kolmogorov–Arnold Network. The name comes from two mathematicians, Andrey Kolmogorov and Vladimir Arnold, whose theorem is the main mathematical inspiration behind the model. The big idea is: instead of putting simple fixed activation functions inside neurons like a normal neural network, KAN puts learnable 1D functions on the connections between neurons
How KAN works?

• every edge has a learnable function,

• those functions are often represented using splines,

• the networks stacks these function-based layer to build more complex behaviour
Press enter or click to view image in full size

d/w normal NN & KANKAN matters because it challenges a very old habit in deep learning: that the main building block must be “linear layers plus fixed activations.” The KAN paper suggests that moving learnable functions from nodes to edges can sometimes give better accuracy with fewer parameters and can make models easier to understand. That is a big deal because smaller models are often easier to train, easier to inspect, and sometimes faster to generalize.
Now why is it popularly known as a glass box model
A glass box model (sometimes called a white box model) is an AI or computer system whose inner workings are completely transparent
KAN is called a glass box because the learned functions on edges can often be plotted and inspected directly. In a normal deep network, weights and activations are buried across many layers, so understanding the model is hard. In KAN, you can sometimes look at the learned curve on an edge and say, “Ah, this edge learned something like sine,” or “this part looks like a quadratic shape”.

• The KAN documentation even shows that after training, the model can sometimes produce a symbolic formula, not just a numeric prediction. That is one reason people say KAN is more interpretable.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A sample diagram of KAN architectureWhy I Chose KAN
While working on my AeroKAN project, I wanted to experiment with something beyond conventional neural networks. Predicting engine degradation involves learning complex patterns from sensor readings, but I also wanted a model that could offer better interpretability.
KAN stood out because of its glass-box nature. Instead of treating the model as a black box, KAN provides a more transparent way to understand how the network learns relationships between features. This made it an exciting choice for a project where understanding the model can be just as valuable as achieving high accuracy.
